text: By prioritizing global image structure over pixel-level details, our approach enables generative modeling that is robust to spatial variations such as rotation and translation. This study presents a framework for high-resolution 3D image synthesis that leverages the latent space of a VQ-GAN to significantly reduce computational overhead. We demonstrate the versatility of our method across four diverse medical imaging datasets, even when constrained by limited sample sizes ($n \leq 1844$). Our model consistently outperforms established GAN benchmarks by producing greater diversity and exhibiting higher resilience to mode collapse. Recognizing that standard quantitative metrics often fail to capture the nuances of medical imaging, we incorporated expert evaluations from experienced radiologists to validate the clinical realism of our results. Finally, we highlight the utility of our synthetic data in low-resource environments: by using synthetic images for self-supervised pre-training of a Swin-UNETR, we achieved substantial performance gains in downstream segmentation tasks at external institutions. These findings underscore the potential of synthetic data to bridge the gap in medical imaging domains where high-quality labeled datasets are notoriously scarce.
